concept.headline: Kotlin separates read-only (listOf, mapOf) from mutable (mutableListOf, mutableMapOf) collections. filter, map, and forEach transform and iterate them.
concept.explanation: Read-only collections expose no mutation methods — but are not deeply immutable (the underlying list can still change if you hold a mutable reference). Mutable collections (MutableList, MutableMap, MutableSet) add add(), remove(), put() etc. The functional operators filter { }, map { }, forEach { }, find { }, any { }, all { } work on all collection types.
keyPoints: filter { condition } returns a new list of matching elements
map { transform } returns a new list of transformed elements

demo.intro: Watch a list being filtered and mapped to produce a new transformed list.

explanation.why: listOf returns an immutable view; the numbers are stored in order
explanation.misconception: listOf does NOT mean the data is frozen in a JVM sense — it means this reference has no add/remove methods. Under the hood it uses Java's Arrays.asList.

explanation.what: nums.filter { it % 2 == 0 } — keep only elements where condition is true
explanation.why: it refers to the current element; it % 2 == 0 is true for even numbers
explanation.intent: Show filter producing a new list — original is unchanged
explanation.misconception: filter does NOT modify nums — it returns a brand-new list. nums still equals [1,2,3,4,5] after this line.

explanation.why: it is each element of evens; it * 2 doubles it
explanation.intent: Show map producing a transformed list of the same size
explanation.misconception: map always returns a list of the SAME SIZE as the input — one output per input. Use filter to reduce size, map to transform.
Java would print the memory address for a raw array. Kotlin collections always print their contents.

explanation.intent: Confirm filter + map pipeline produced expected result
explanation.misconception: These operations can be chained: nums.filter { ... }.map { ... } — no need for intermediate variables

prediction.question:
What is the output?

val words = listOf("apple", "banana", "avocado")
val aWords = words.filter { it.startsWith("a") }
println(aWords.size)
prediction.explanation: filter keeps 'apple' and 'avocado' (both start with 'a') — 'banana' is excluded. aWords.size = 2.

explanation.afterDemo: Kotlin's collection API is functional and composable. filter, map, forEach, find, any, all chain together cleanly. The read-only / mutable split makes APIs safer — when a function returns a List<T>, callers know they can't accidentally mutate it. Use mutableListOf only when you genuinely need to add/remove elements.
keyTakeaways: listOf → read-only; mutableListOf → add/remove. Both expose filter/map/forEach.
filter { predicate } — returns new list of elements matching the condition.
map { transform } — returns new list of transformed elements, same size as input.

practice.prompt: Given listOf("cat", "dog", "elephant", "ant", "bear"), filter words with length <= 3 and map them to uppercase. Print the result.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! cat, dog, ant pass the length filter; then each is uppercased.
retryHints: filter { it.length <= 3 } keeps short words: cat, dog, ant
map { it.uppercase() } transforms each to uppercase

summary.title: Collections — Complete
takeaways: listOf / mapOf / setOf — read-only. mutableListOf / mutableMapOf — mutable.
filter { } narrows a list; map { } transforms each element into a new list.
Chain operations: list.filter { }.map { }.forEach { } — clean functional pipelines.
